concepts.label: Fondation Louis Vuitton
concepts.definition: Art museum in Paris designed by Frank Gehry, opened 2014, glass sails resembling a ship

concepts.label: The Broad
concepts.definition: Contemporary art museum in Los Angeles designed by Diller Scofidio + Renfro, opened 2015, honeycomb veil facade

concepts.label: Concrete
concepts.definition: Composite building material made from cement, water, and aggregates, most widely used construction material worldwide

concepts.label: Reinforced Concrete
concepts.definition: Concrete embedded with steel reinforcement bars (rebar) to improve tensile strength, enabling larger structural spans

concepts.label: Prestressed Concrete
concepts.definition: Concrete with pre-tensioned or post-tensioned steel tendons to improve performance under service loads

concepts.label: Steel
concepts.definition: Iron alloy with carbon used in construction for structural frameworks, known for high tensile strength and ductility

concepts.label: Structural Steel
concepts.definition: Steel shaped into specific profiles (I-beams, channels, angles) for use as load-bearing structural members

concepts.label: Stainless Steel
concepts.definition: Corrosion-resistant steel alloy containing chromium, used in architecture for cladding, fixtures, and structural elements

concepts.label: Corten Steel
concepts.definition: Weathering steel that forms a stable rust-like patina, used as architectural cladding for its natural appearance

concepts.label: Brick
concepts.definition: Block of fired clay or concrete used in masonry construction, one of the oldest building materials

concepts.label: Adobe
concepts.definition: Building material made from earth, water, and organic materials like straw, sun-dried into bricks

concepts.label: Rammed Earth
concepts.definition: Building technique using compacted layers of earth in formwork to create dense, durable walls

concepts.label: Timber
concepts.definition: Wood processed for use in construction, one of the oldest and most sustainable building materials

concepts.label: Cross-Laminated Timber
concepts.definition: Engineered wood product with layers glued at right angles, enabling large-scale timber construction

concepts.label: Glulam
concepts.definition: Glue-laminated timber made by bonding wood layers, used for large spans and curved structural members

concepts.label: Plywood
concepts.definition: Engineered wood made from thin veneer layers glued with alternating grain directions for strength

concepts.label: Bamboo
concepts.definition: Fast-growing grass used as construction material, high tensile strength comparable to steel, sustainable

concepts.label: Granite
concepts.definition: Igneous rock used in construction for facades, countertops, and paving, extremely hard and durable

concepts.label: Marble
concepts.definition: Metamorphic rock prized in architecture for its beauty, used for facades, floors, and sculpture since antiquity

concepts.label: Limestone
concepts.definition: Sedimentary rock widely used in construction, forms the basis of cement and is used as building stone

concepts.label: Sandstone
concepts.definition: Sedimentary rock used in construction for its workability and warm color, common in historic buildings

concepts.label: Slate
concepts.definition: Fine-grained metamorphic rock used for roofing, flooring, and cladding, splits into thin durable sheets

concepts.label: Travertine
concepts.definition: Form of limestone deposited by mineral springs, used as decorative stone in building facades and floors

concepts.label: Basalt
concepts.definition: Dark volcanic rock used in construction for road base, aggregate, and occasionally as building stone

concepts.label: Glass
concepts.definition: Transparent silica-based material used in windows, facades, and structural elements in architecture

concepts.label: Tempered Glass
concepts.definition: Safety glass treated by thermal or chemical processes, 4-5 times stronger than regular glass, shatters into small pieces

concepts.label: Laminated Glass
concepts.definition: Safety glass with interlayer holding shards together when broken, used for structural glazing and safety applications

concepts.label: Low-E Glass
concepts.definition: Glass with thin metallic coating reflecting infrared radiation, improving thermal performance of windows

concepts.label: ETFE
concepts.definition: Ethylene tetrafluoroethylene polymer used as lightweight transparent building envelope, alternative to glass

concepts.label: Polycarbonate
concepts.definition: Thermoplastic used in architecture for translucent panels, skylights, and greenhouse cladding, impact-resistant

concepts.label: Aluminum
concepts.definition: Lightweight corrosion-resistant metal used in construction for curtain walls, window frames, and cladding

concepts.label: Copper Cladding
concepts.definition: Architectural copper sheeting that develops green patina over time, used for roofing and facades

concepts.label: Zinc Cladding
concepts.definition: Architectural zinc sheeting developing a protective blue-gray patina, used for roofing and wall cladding

concepts.label: Titanium Cladding
concepts.definition: Lightweight corrosion-resistant metal cladding, famously used on the Guggenheim Museum Bilbao

concepts.label: Lead Sheet
concepts.definition: Dense metal sheet traditionally used for roofing, flashing, and waterproofing in historic buildings

concepts.label: Ceramic Tile
concepts.definition: Fired clay tiles used for flooring, walls, and decoration, available in glazed and unglazed varieties

concepts.label: Terracotta
concepts.definition: Fired clay material used for building facades, roof tiles, and decorative elements, reddish-brown color

concepts.label: Portland Cement
concepts.definition: Most common type of cement, hydraulic binding agent mixed with water and aggregates to make concrete

concepts.label: Mortar
concepts.definition: Paste of cement, sand, and water used to bind bricks, stones, and other masonry units

concepts.label: Plaster
concepts.definition: Building material for coating walls and ceilings, made from gypsum, lime, or cement mixed with water

concepts.label: Stucco
concepts.definition: Exterior plaster finish made from cement, sand, lime, and water, applied wet to walls and ceilings

concepts.label: Gypsum Board
concepts.definition: Panel of gypsum plaster between paper facers, standard interior wall and ceiling material (drywall)

concepts.label: Fiberglass Insulation
concepts.definition: Insulation material made from fine glass fibers, commonly used in walls, attics, and HVAC ducts

concepts.label: Mineral Wool
concepts.definition: Insulation material made from molten rock or slag spun into fibers, fire-resistant and sound-absorbing

concepts.label: Expanded Polystyrene
concepts.definition: Lightweight rigid foam insulation (EPS) used in walls, roofs, and foundations, good thermal resistance

concepts.label: Polyurethane Foam
concepts.definition: Spray or rigid foam insulation with high R-value per inch, used in roofs, walls, and foundations

concepts.label: Asphalt Shingles
concepts.definition: Most common roofing material in North America, made from fiberglass mat coated with asphalt and granules

concepts.label: Clay Roof Tiles
concepts.definition: Durable roofing material made from fired clay, common in Mediterranean and tropical architecture

concepts.label: Green Roof
concepts.definition: Roof system covered with vegetation and growing medium, providing insulation and stormwater management

concepts.label: Thatch
concepts.definition: Traditional roofing material of dried straw, water reed, or palm leaves, providing natural insulation

concepts.label: Fiber Cement Board
concepts.definition: Composite building material of cement reinforced with cellulose fibers, used for siding and facades

concepts.label: Oriented Strand Board
concepts.definition: Engineered wood panel made from compressed wood strands, used for sheathing, subflooring, and roofing

concepts.label: Medium-Density Fiberboard
concepts.definition: Engineered wood product made from wood fibers bonded with resin, used for furniture and cabinetry

concepts.label: Cork
concepts.definition: Natural material from cork oak bark used for flooring, insulation, and acoustic panels, renewable

concepts.label: Hempcrete
concepts.definition: Bio-composite building material made from hemp hurds and lime, used for insulation and non-load-bearing walls

concepts.label: Mycelium Building Material
concepts.definition: Emerging bio-material grown from mushroom root structures, used as insulation and packaging, fully biodegradable

concepts.label: Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer
concepts.definition: Lightweight high-strength composite used in structural reinforcement and architectural elements

concepts.label: Geopolymer Concrete
concepts.definition: Alternative to Portland cement concrete using industrial waste like fly ash, lower carbon footprint

concepts.label: Self-Healing Concrete
concepts.definition: Concrete containing bacteria or capsules that activate to seal cracks automatically when they form

concepts.label: Transparent Concrete
concepts.definition: Concrete embedded with optical fibers allowing light to pass through, used for decorative architectural elements

concepts.label: Aerogel Insulation
concepts.definition: Ultra-lightweight silica-based insulation with extremely low thermal conductivity, used in high-performance buildings

concepts.label: Photovoltaic Glass
concepts.definition: Building-integrated glass panels that generate electricity from sunlight while maintaining transparency

concepts.label: Electrochromic Glass
concepts.definition: Smart glass that changes tint when electrical voltage is applied, controlling solar heat gain and glare

concepts.label: Gabion Wall
concepts.definition: Wire mesh cage filled with rocks or concrete, used for retaining walls, erosion control, and decorative elements

concepts.label: Ferrocement
concepts.definition: Thin reinforced concrete using wire mesh and cement mortar, lightweight and versatile construction technique

concepts.label: Cob
concepts.definition: Natural building material made from subsoil, water, organic material, and sometimes lime, hand-formed into walls

concepts.label: Wattle and Daub
concepts.definition: Ancient building technique using woven lattice of wooden strips (wattle) coated with mud mixture (daub)

concepts.label: Rubble Stone
concepts.definition: Rough, uncut stones used in masonry construction, often with mortar, common in vernacular architecture

concepts.label: Ashlar
concepts.definition: Finely cut and dressed stone blocks used in masonry with thin mortar joints, creating smooth facades

concepts.label: Precast Concrete
concepts.definition: Concrete elements cast in reusable molds at a factory, then transported and assembled on-site

concepts.label: Tensile Fabric
concepts.definition: Membrane material (PTFE, PVC-coated polyester) stretched over structural framework for lightweight roofs

concepts.label: Structural Insulated Panel
concepts.definition: Prefabricated building panel of rigid foam core between structural facings, high insulation and strength

concepts.label: Mass Timber
concepts.definition: Category of large-scale engineered wood products (CLT, glulam, NLT) enabling tall timber buildings

concepts.label: Reclaimed Wood
concepts.definition: Salvaged timber from old structures repurposed in new construction, valued for character and sustainability

concepts.label: Recycled Steel
concepts.definition: Steel manufactured from scrap metal, reducing energy use and CO2 emissions compared to virgin steel

concepts.label: Fly Ash Concrete
concepts.definition: Concrete using coal combustion byproduct (fly ash) as partial cement replacement, improving durability

concepts.label: Pervious Concrete
concepts.definition: Porous concrete allowing water to pass through, used for stormwater management in pavements and walkways

concepts.label: Engineered Stone
concepts.definition: Composite material made from crushed stone and resin, used for countertops and cladding

concepts.label: Linoleum
concepts.definition: Flooring material made from linseed oil, pine resin, wood flour, and cork dust on canvas backing

concepts.label: Terrazzo
concepts.definition: Composite flooring of marble, quartz, granite, or glass chips set in cement or resin, polished smooth

concepts.label: Tadelakt
concepts.definition: Traditional Moroccan lime plaster technique creating waterproof, polished surfaces, used in hammams

concepts.label: Rattan
concepts.definition: Palm plant stems used for furniture, baskets, and architectural screens, flexible and sustainable

concepts.label: Golden Ratio
concepts.definition: Mathematical ratio (~1.618:1) used in architecture and design for aesthetically pleasing proportions

concepts.label: Form Follows Function
concepts.definition: Design principle stating that the shape of a building should derive from its intended purpose, coined by Louis Sullivan

concepts.label: Less Is More
concepts.definition: Design philosophy associated with Mies van der Rohe and minimalism, advocating simplicity and restraint

concepts.label: Five Points of Architecture
concepts.definition: Le Corbusier's principles: pilotis, free facade, open floor plan, ribbon windows, and roof garden

concepts.label: Modulor
concepts.definition: Le Corbusier's anthropometric scale of proportions based on human body measurements and the golden ratio

concepts.label: Feng Shui
concepts.definition: Chinese philosophical system of spatial arrangement harmonizing individuals with their environment through energy flow

concepts.label: Wabi-Sabi
concepts.definition: Japanese aesthetic centered on acceptance of transience and imperfection, influencing design with natural simplicity

concepts.label: Universal Design
concepts.definition: Design approach creating environments usable by all people regardless of age, ability, or status

concepts.label: Human Scale
concepts.definition: Design principle ensuring built environments relate proportionally to the human body and perception

concepts.label: Symmetry in Design
concepts.definition: Balance achieved through mirrored arrangement of elements, fundamental principle in classical architecture

concepts.label: Asymmetric Balance
concepts.definition: Visual balance achieved through unequal but visually weighted arrangement of elements

concepts.label: Rhythm in Architecture
concepts.definition: Repetition and pattern of architectural elements creating visual movement and coherence

concepts.label: Proportion in Architecture
concepts.definition: Harmonious relationship between parts and whole in building design, critical to aesthetic appeal

concepts.label: Hierarchy in Design
concepts.definition: Organization of elements by importance through size, color, placement, or contrast

concepts.label: Contrast in Design
concepts.definition: Juxtaposition of different elements (light/dark, rough/smooth, large/small) to create visual interest

concepts.label: Unity in Design
concepts.definition: Coherent relationship between all design elements creating a complete, harmonious whole

concepts.label: Emphasis in Design
concepts.definition: Creating focal points in a design through contrast, placement, or isolation of elements

concepts.label: Negative Space
concepts.definition: Empty or open space around and between design elements, as important as positive forms

concepts.label: Passive Solar Design
concepts.definition: Building design strategy using the sun's energy for heating and lighting without mechanical systems

concepts.label: Daylighting
concepts.definition: Practice of using natural light to illuminate building interiors, reducing energy use and improving well-being

concepts.label: Natural Ventilation
concepts.definition: Using wind and thermal buoyancy to ventilate buildings without mechanical systems, reducing energy consumption

concepts.label: Thermal Mass
concepts.definition: Building materials' capacity to absorb, store, and release heat, moderating temperature fluctuations

concepts.label: Cradle to Cradle
concepts.definition: Design framework treating all materials as nutrients in biological or technical cycles, eliminating waste

concepts.label: Frank Lloyd Wright
concepts.definition: American architect (1867-1959), pioneer of organic architecture, designed Fallingwater and Guggenheim New York

concepts.label: Le Corbusier
concepts.definition: Swiss-French architect (1887-1965), pioneer of modern architecture, designed Villa Savoye, Ronchamp Chapel, and Chandigarh

concepts.label: Ludwig Mies van der Rohe
concepts.definition: German-American architect (1886-1969), pioneer of modernism, designed Farnsworth House, Seagram Building, Barcelona Pavilion

concepts.label: Frank Gehry
concepts.definition: Canadian-American architect (b.1929), known for deconstructivist buildings including Guggenheim Bilbao and Walt Disney Concert Hall

concepts.label: Zaha Hadid
concepts.definition: Iraqi-British architect (1950-2016), first woman to receive Pritzker Prize (2004), known for fluid parametric forms

concepts.label: Rem Koolhaas
concepts.definition: Dutch architect (b.1944), founder of OMA, Pritzker Prize 2000, designed CCTV Headquarters and Seattle Central Library

concepts.label: Tadao Ando
concepts.definition: Japanese self-taught architect (b.1941), Pritzker Prize 1995, known for concrete, light, and water in minimal forms

concepts.label: Renzo Piano
concepts.definition: Italian architect (b.1937), Pritzker Prize 1998, co-designed Centre Pompidou, designed The Shard and Whitney Museum

concepts.label: Norman Foster
concepts.definition: British architect (b.1935), Pritzker Prize 1999, designed 30 St Mary Axe, Reichstag dome, Apple Park

concepts.label: I.M. Pei
concepts.definition: Chinese-American architect (1917-2019), Pritzker Prize 1983, designed Louvre Pyramid and Bank of China Tower

concepts.label: Oscar Niemeyer
concepts.definition: Brazilian architect (1907-2012), Pritzker Prize 1988, designed Brasília's major buildings with sweeping curves

concepts.label: Louis Kahn
concepts.definition: American architect (1901-1974), known for monumental forms and use of light, designed Salk Institute and Kimbell Art Museum

concepts.label: Philip Johnson
concepts.definition: American architect (1906-2005), first Pritzker Prize 1979, designed Glass House and AT&T Building

concepts.label: Eero Saarinen
concepts.definition: Finnish-American architect (1910-1961), designed TWA Terminal, Gateway Arch, and Dulles Airport

concepts.label: Alvar Aalto
concepts.definition: Finnish architect (1898-1976), pioneer of Nordic modernism, humanized modern architecture with organic forms

concepts.label: Walter Gropius
concepts.definition: German-American architect (1883-1969), founder of the Bauhaus school, pioneer of modern architecture

concepts.label: Antoni Gaudí
concepts.definition: Catalan architect (1852-1926), master of Art Nouveau, designed Sagrada Familia, Casa Batlló, and Park Güell

concepts.label: Santiago Calatrava
concepts.definition: Spanish architect and engineer (b.1951), known for white skeletal structures, designed Oculus NYC and City of Arts and Sciences

concepts.label: Daniel Libeskind
concepts.definition: Polish-American architect (b.1946), designed Jewish Museum Berlin and master plan for One World Trade Center

concepts.label: Bjarke Ingels
concepts.definition: Danish architect (b.1974), founder of BIG, known for playful pragmatic designs like CopenHill and 2 World Trade Center

concepts.label: Jean Nouvel
concepts.definition: French architect (b.1945), Pritzker Prize 2008, designed Institut du Monde Arabe and Louvre Abu Dhabi

concepts.label: Kengo Kuma
concepts.definition: Japanese architect (b.1954), known for natural materials and anti-monumental design, designed Japan National Stadium

concepts.label: Peter Zumthor
concepts.definition: Swiss architect (b.1943), Pritzker Prize 2009, known for sensory atmospheric spaces, designed Therme Vals

concepts.label: Shigeru Ban
concepts.definition: Japanese architect (b.1957), Pritzker Prize 2014, known for paper tube and cardboard architecture for disaster relief

concepts.label: David Adjaye
concepts.definition: Ghanaian-British architect (b.1966), designed National Museum of African American History and Culture

concepts.label: Herzog & de Meuron
concepts.definition: Swiss architectural firm (founded 1978), Pritzker Prize 2001, designed Tate Modern, Beijing National Stadium, Elbphilharmonie

concepts.label: Toyo Ito
concepts.definition: Japanese architect (b.1941), Pritzker Prize 2013, known for lightweight, optimistic architecture

concepts.label: Fumihiko Maki
concepts.definition: Japanese architect (b.1928), Pritzker Prize 1993, known for elegant modernist buildings using light and space

concepts.label: Rafael Moneo
concepts.definition: Spanish architect (b.1937), Pritzker Prize 1996, designed Museum of Roman Art Mérida and Cathedral of Our Lady of the Angels

concepts.label: Álvaro Siza
concepts.definition: Portuguese architect (b.1933), Pritzker Prize 1992, known for white minimalist buildings sensitive to context

concepts.label: Eduardo Souto de Moura
concepts.definition: Portuguese architect (b.1952), Pritzker Prize 2011, known for stone and concrete modernist buildings

concepts.label: Richard Meier
concepts.definition: American architect (b.1934), Pritzker Prize 1984, known for white geometric buildings like Getty Center

concepts.label: Richard Rogers
concepts.definition: British-Italian architect (1933-2021), Pritzker Prize 2007, co-designed Centre Pompidou and Lloyd's building

concepts.label: Thom Mayne
concepts.definition: American architect (b.1944), Pritzker Prize 2005, founder of Morphosis, known for bold deconstructivist works

concepts.label: Glenn Murcutt
concepts.definition: Australian architect (b.1936), Pritzker Prize 2002, known for environmentally sensitive minimal houses

concepts.label: Balkrishna Doshi
concepts.definition: Indian architect (1927-2023), Pritzker Prize 2018, designed Indian Institute of Management Bangalore and Aranya housing

concepts.label: Arata Isozaki
concepts.definition: Japanese architect (1931-2022), Pritzker Prize 2019, designed Museum of Contemporary Art Los Angeles

concepts.label: Diébédo Francis Kéré
concepts.definition: Burkinabé-German architect (b.1965), Pritzker Prize 2022, known for community-focused sustainable buildings in Africa

concepts.label: Alejandro Aravena
concepts.definition: Chilean architect (b.1967), Pritzker Prize 2016, known for incremental social housing designs

concepts.label: Wang Shu
concepts.definition: Chinese architect (b.1963), Pritzker Prize 2012, known for using recycled materials and traditional Chinese techniques

concepts.label: Christian de Portzamparc
concepts.definition: French architect (b.1944), Pritzker Prize 1994, designed Cité de la Musique and One57 tower

concepts.label: Sverre Fehn
concepts.definition: Norwegian architect (1924-2009), Pritzker Prize 1997, poetic modernism integrating nature and structure

concepts.label: Paulo Mendes da Rocha
concepts.definition: Brazilian architect (1928-2021), Pritzker Prize 2006, known for bold concrete brutalist structures

concepts.label: Robert Venturi
concepts.definition: American architect (1925-2018), Pritzker Prize 1991, father of postmodernism, wrote Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture

concepts.label: Gottfried Semper
concepts.definition: German architect (1803-1879), designed Semperoper Dresden, theorist on the relationship of architecture and society

concepts.label: Andrea Palladio
concepts.definition: Italian Renaissance architect (1508-1580), hugely influential, designed Villa Rotonda, wrote Four Books of Architecture

concepts.label: Filippo Brunelleschi
concepts.definition: Italian architect (1377-1446), engineered the dome of Florence Cathedral, pioneer of Renaissance architecture

concepts.label: Christopher Wren
concepts.definition: English architect (1632-1723), designed St Paul's Cathedral London and 52 London churches after the Great Fire

concepts.label: Karl Friedrich Schinkel
concepts.definition: Prussian architect (1781-1841), designed Altes Museum Berlin, pioneer of Neoclassical architecture in Germany

concepts.label: Louis Sullivan
concepts.definition: American architect (1856-1924), father of the skyscraper, coined 'form follows function', mentor to Frank Lloyd Wright

concepts.label: Kenzo Tange
concepts.definition: Japanese architect (1913-2005), Pritzker Prize 1987, bridged traditional Japanese and modernist architecture

concepts.label: Moshe Safdie
concepts.definition: Israeli-Canadian architect (b.1938), designed Habitat 67, Marina Bay Sands, and Jewel Changi Airport

concepts.label: Snøhetta
concepts.definition: Norwegian architectural firm founded 1989, designed Oslo Opera House and Bibliotheca Alexandrina

concepts.label: OMA
concepts.definition: Office for Metropolitan Architecture, founded by Rem Koolhaas in 1975, designed CCTV Headquarters and Seattle Central Library

concepts.label: SOM
concepts.definition: Skidmore, Owings & Merrill, American architectural firm founded 1936, designed Willis Tower and Lever House

concepts.label: Diller Scofidio + Renfro
concepts.definition: American architectural firm known for The High Line, The Shed, and The Broad museum

concepts.label: MVRDV
concepts.definition: Dutch architectural firm founded 1993, known for innovative stacked and pixelated building designs

concepts.label: Lacaton & Vassal
concepts.definition: French architectural duo, Pritzker Prize 2021, known for generous, economical, ecological renovations

concepts.label: Jørn Utzon
concepts.definition: Danish architect (1918-2008), Pritzker Prize 2003, designed Sydney Opera House

concepts.label: Lina Bo Bardi
concepts.definition: Italian-Brazilian architect (1914-1992), designed MASP São Paulo, champion of social architecture

concepts.label: Carlo Scarpa
concepts.definition: Italian architect (1906-1978), master of detail and material, designed Castelvecchio Museum renovation and Brion Cemetery

concepts.label: Kisho Kurokawa
concepts.definition: Japanese architect (1934-2007), Metabolist movement co-founder, designed Nakagin Capsule Tower

concepts.label: Gordon Bunshaft
concepts.definition: American architect (1909-1990), Pritzker Prize 1988, designed Lever House and Beinecke Rare Book Library

concepts.label: Buckminster Fuller
concepts.definition: American architect and inventor (1895-1983), developed geodesic dome and Dymaxion concepts

concepts.label: Frei Otto
concepts.definition: German architect (1925-2015), Pritzker Prize 2015, pioneer of lightweight tensile and membrane structures

concepts.label: Cesar Pelli
concepts.definition: Argentine-American architect (1926-2019), designed Petronas Towers and World Financial Center

concepts.label: Ieoh Ming Pei
concepts.definition: Chinese-American architect (1917-2019), Pritzker 1983, designed Louvre Pyramid and Museum of Islamic Art Doha

concepts.label: Thomas Heatherwick
concepts.definition: British designer (b.1970), designed UK Pavilion at Shanghai Expo 2010, Vessel NYC, and new Google HQ

concepts.label: Ma Yansong
concepts.definition: Chinese architect (b.1975), founder of MAD Architects, known for futuristic organic forms

concepts.label: Jeanne Gang
concepts.definition: American architect (b.1964), founder of Studio Gang, designed Aqua Tower Chicago with undulating balconies

concepts.label: Tatiana Bilbao
concepts.definition: Mexican architect (b.1972), known for sustainable social housing and cultural buildings

concepts.label: Peter Eisenman
concepts.definition: American architect (b.1932), deconstructivist theorist, designed Memorial to the Murdered Jews of Europe

concepts.label: Bernard Tschumi
concepts.definition: Swiss-French architect (b.1944), designed Parc de la Villette and New Acropolis Museum

concepts.label: Coop Himmelb(l)au
concepts.definition: Austrian architectural firm founded 1968, deconstructivist pioneers, designed BMW Welt and Musée des Confluences

concepts.label: Steven Holl
concepts.definition: American architect (b.1947), known for experiential architecture using light and water, designed Kiasma Helsinki

concepts.label: David Chipperfield
concepts.definition: British architect (b.1953), Pritzker Prize 2023, designed Neues Museum Berlin renovation

concepts.label: Kazuyo Sejima
concepts.definition: Japanese architect (b.1956), co-founder of SANAA, Pritzker Prize 2010, known for transparent minimalist buildings

concepts.label: Ryue Nishizawa
concepts.definition: Japanese architect (b.1966), co-founder of SANAA, Pritzker Prize 2010, designed Teshima Art Museum

concepts.label: Riken Yamamoto
concepts.definition: Japanese architect (b.1945), Pritzker Prize 2024, known for public and community-focused architecture

concepts.label: Mid-Century Modern Furniture
concepts.definition: Furniture style (1945-1970) featuring clean lines, organic curves, and mixed materials like molded plywood and fiberglass

concepts.label: Art Deco Furniture
concepts.definition: Furniture style (1920s-1930s) featuring geometric forms, exotic materials, lacquer, and streamlined luxury

concepts.label: Scandinavian Modern Furniture
concepts.definition: Nordic furniture design emphasizing simplicity, functionality, minimalism, and natural materials like wood

concepts.label: Bauhaus Furniture
concepts.definition: Furniture from the Bauhaus school featuring tubular steel, geometric forms, and industrial production methods

concepts.label: Arts and Crafts Furniture
concepts.definition: Late 19th/early 20th-century furniture emphasizing handcraft, simple construction, and natural materials

concepts.label: Shaker Furniture
concepts.definition: American furniture style known for extreme simplicity, utility, and quality craftsmanship, no ornamentation

concepts.label: Queen Anne Furniture
concepts.definition: Early 18th-century English furniture style featuring cabriole legs, pad feet, and vase-shaped splats

concepts.label: Chippendale Furniture
concepts.definition: 18th-century English furniture style by Thomas Chippendale featuring elaborate carved mahogany pieces

concepts.label: Louis XVI Furniture
concepts.definition: French Neoclassical furniture style featuring straight lines, geometric forms, and classical motifs

concepts.label: Empire Furniture
concepts.definition: Early 19th-century French furniture style inspired by Napoleonic campaigns, featuring Egyptian and Roman motifs

concepts.label: Victorian Furniture
concepts.definition: 19th-century furniture featuring heavy ornamentation, dark woods, tufted upholstery, and eclectic styles

concepts.label: Art Nouveau Furniture
concepts.definition: Furniture (1890-1910) featuring flowing organic curves, nature-inspired motifs, and innovative materials

concepts.label: Memphis Group Furniture
concepts.definition: 1980s Italian design movement led by Ettore Sottsass featuring bold colors, geometric shapes, and kitsch materials

concepts.label: Japanese Furniture
concepts.definition: Traditional Japanese furniture design featuring low platforms, futons, zabuton cushions, and minimal pieces

concepts.label: Biedermeier Furniture
concepts.definition: Central European furniture style (1815-1848) featuring simple, elegant forms in light-colored woods

concepts.label: Eames Lounge Chair
concepts.definition: Iconic 1956 chair by Charles and Ray Eames featuring molded plywood shells and leather cushions

concepts.label: Barcelona Chair
concepts.definition: Iconic 1929 chair by Mies van der Rohe featuring chrome X-frame and leather cushions, designed for German Pavilion

concepts.label: Wassily Chair
concepts.definition: Tubular steel chair designed by Marcel Breuer at the Bauhaus in 1925-1926, inspired by bicycle handlebars

concepts.label: Egg Chair
concepts.definition: 1958 chair by Arne Jacobsen with curved shell form for the SAS Royal Hotel in Copenhagen

concepts.label: Tulip Chair
concepts.definition: 1956 pedestal chair by Eero Saarinen with single leg, designed to eliminate clutter of chair legs

concepts.label: Wegner Wishbone Chair
concepts.definition: CH24 chair by Hans Wegner (1949) featuring Y-shaped back, steam-bent frame, and paper cord seat

concepts.label: Panton Chair
concepts.definition: 1967 cantilevered single-piece molded plastic chair by Verner Panton, first of its kind

concepts.label: Minimalist Interior Design
concepts.definition: Interior design approach using sparse furnishings, clean lines, neutral colors, and uncluttered spaces

concepts.label: Scandinavian Interior Design
concepts.definition: Nordic-inspired interiors featuring light colors, natural materials, functionality, and hygge comfort

concepts.label: Industrial Interior Design
concepts.definition: Interior style inspired by factories and warehouses, featuring exposed brick, metal, pipes, and raw materials

concepts.label: Bohemian Interior Design

concepts.label: Inclusive Design
concepts.definition: Design methodology ensuring products and environments work for the widest range of people possible

concepts.label: Emotional Design
concepts.definition: Design approach (Don Norman) creating products evoking positive emotions through visceral, behavioral, and reflective levels

concepts.label: Dieter Rams
concepts.definition: German industrial designer (b.1932), Braun's chief designer, created ten principles of good design influencing Apple

concepts.label: Charles and Ray Eames
concepts.definition: American husband-wife design duo, pioneered molded plywood and fiberglass furniture, icons of mid-century design

concepts.label: Arne Jacobsen
concepts.definition: Danish architect and designer (1902-1971), designed Egg Chair and Swan Chair, pioneer of Danish Modern

concepts.label: Hans Wegner
concepts.definition: Danish furniture designer (1914-2007), designed over 500 chairs including the Wishbone and Round Chair

concepts.label: Verner Panton
concepts.definition: Danish designer (1926-1998), created first single-piece molded plastic chair and psychedelic interiors

concepts.label: Ettore Sottsass
concepts.definition: Italian architect and designer (1917-2007), founder of Memphis Group, designed Olivetti Valentine typewriter

concepts.label: William Morris
concepts.definition: English artist and designer (1834-1896), leader of Arts and Crafts movement, wallpaper and textile designer

concepts.label: Marcel Breuer
concepts.definition: Hungarian-American architect and designer (1902-1981), Bauhaus master, designed Wassily Chair and Whitney Museum

concepts.label: Philippe Starck
concepts.definition: French designer (b.1949), known for accessible democratic design in furniture, interiors, and products

concepts.label: Jonathan Ive
concepts.definition: British-American designer (b.1967), Apple's chief design officer, designed iMac, iPod, iPhone, shaping modern tech aesthetics

concepts.label: Pritzker Architecture Prize
concepts.definition: Annual award considered architecture's highest honor, established 1979, recognizing lifetime achievement

concepts.label: RIBA Gold Medal
concepts.definition: Royal Institute of British Architects medal given annually for significant contribution to international architecture

concepts.label: AIA Gold Medal
concepts.definition: American Institute of Architects' highest award, recognizing significant body of work with lasting influence

concepts.label: Flying Buttress
concepts.definition: Architectural support transferring thrust from an upper wall to an outer pier, enabling tall Gothic walls with large windows

concepts.label: Pointed Arch
concepts.definition: Arch with a pointed apex, characteristic of Gothic architecture, distributing weight more efficiently than round arches

concepts.label: Ribbed Vault
concepts.definition: Ceiling vault with diagonal arched ribs of stone, characteristic of Gothic architecture, directing forces to pillars

concepts.label: Dome
concepts.definition: Hemispherical vault forming a roof or ceiling, one of the oldest architectural structural forms

concepts.label: Minaret
concepts.definition: Tall slender tower of a mosque from which the call to prayer is announced, key element of Islamic architecture

concepts.label: Colonnade
concepts.definition: Row of columns supporting an entablature or series of arches, used in classical and neoclassical architecture

concepts.label: Cantilever
concepts.definition: Structural element anchored at one end, projecting horizontally without external bracing, used for balconies and roofs

concepts.label: Curtain Wall
concepts.definition: Non-structural exterior wall system, typically glass and metal, hung on the building frame like a curtain

concepts.label: Geodesic Dome
concepts.definition: Spherical thin-shell structure of triangulated elements, developed by Buckminster Fuller, extremely efficient

concepts.label: Diagrid Structure
concepts.definition: Structural framework of intersecting diagonal members, creating a diamond-shaped grid pattern on building exteriors

concepts.label: Atrium
concepts.definition: Large open central space in a building, often multi-story, bringing natural light into interior

concepts.label: Clerestory
concepts.definition: High section of wall with windows above adjacent rooflines, allowing natural light into upper spaces

concepts.label: Corbel
concepts.definition: Structural piece of stone, wood, or metal jutting from a wall to carry weight above it

concepts.label: Pilotis
concepts.definition: Support columns or stilts raising a building above ground level, championed by Le Corbusier

concepts.label: Muqarnas
concepts.definition: Ornamental vaulting in Islamic architecture, honeycomb-like arrangement of small niches creating stalactite forms

concepts.label: Tracery
concepts.definition: Ornamental stonework in Gothic architecture, forming patterns in windows, especially with stained glass

concepts.label: Rose Window
concepts.definition: Circular window with tracery radiating from center, found in Gothic cathedrals, featuring stained glass

concepts.label: Gargoyle
concepts.definition: Carved stone figure projecting from building, serving as rain spout while warding off evil spirits

concepts.label: Portico
concepts.definition: Porch or entrance supported by columns, often with a pediment, characteristic of classical architecture

concepts.label: Pediment
concepts.definition: Triangular gable end above a portico or facade in classical architecture, often decorated with sculpture

concepts.label: Entablature
concepts.definition: Horizontal element in classical architecture resting on columns, comprising architrave, frieze, and cornice

concepts.label: Cornice
concepts.definition: Projecting horizontal mol
